sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: World Action Models (WAMs) are embodied predictive-action models that make a forecast of the future available to action. Recent WAMs repurpose large video generation models, and a parallel line relies on language or vision-language backbones without a video-generation core. This rapid expansion has blurred the boundary among broad world models, video generation models, action-grounded video world models, Vision-Language-Action policies, and WAMs. This survey gives the field a common account. It first clarifies these boundaries, then organizes existing works through two complementary views. The first view asks what each method is required to generate, spanning rendered futures, latent futures, and video-generation-free action reasoning. The second view decomposes each method by predictive substrate, backbone, action coupling, and deployment regime. This anatomy supports a unified discussion of interactability, causality, persistence, physical plausibility, and generalization, followed by data, evaluation, and open challenges. Across these axes, a consistent design pattern emerges: WAMs are not simply video generators with action heads, but predictive-action methods whose design choices trade representational richness against compute, memory, latency, and action-label cost. The field is moving toward methods that generate less of the future while preserving what control requires. The survey homepage is available at https://world-action-models.github.io/.

sections.title: 1 Introduction
sections.content: 17 18 19 20 21 21 21 23 24 25 26 26 27 27 27 28 29 29 32 32 33 34 34 35 36 36 38 39 39 39 40 40 41 41 41 42 The long-standing goal of embodied AI is to build agents that perceive, reason, and act in unstructured physical environments. Over the past two years the field has moved toward policies that do more than react to the current observation: they also anticipate how the world may change before choosing what to do. The trend began with Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models, which repurpose pretrained vision-language models to map an observation and an instruction directly to an action. Works such as RT-2 [12], OpenVLA [78], and π0 [11] showed that the semantic knowledge accumulated during internet-scale pretraining can be grounded into robotic behavior, generalizing across instructions, objects, and embodiments with modest fine-tuning. standard VLA, however, never models how the environment changes under its own intervention. It learns an observation-to-action mapping with no explicit account of physics, contact, or perspective change. This omission limits the policys ability to reason about consequences before acting. 2 rapidly growing body of work now equips such policy with an explicit predictive component drawn from world models. Large-scale pretrained video generation models serve as major source of such components for predicting future dynamics, but they are not the only choice among existing works. Despite their variety, these works fall into three recognizable streams, distinguished by how far the model carries its prediction before it decodes an action. The first stream adopts video generation backbone all the way to pixels and then decodes action from the rendered future, as in UniPi [29] and the video-plan policies that followed [30, 81]. The second keeps the same backbone but halts before pixel decoding, recovering action from intermediate latents, flow fields, or masks, as in VPP [62] and later latent-space foresight [132, 181]. The third forgoes the video generator entirely and predicts in language-token, audio, or joint-embedding space, as in DUST [171], Audio-WM [194], and latent-dynamics policies over frozen vision features [112]. Despite these differences, the three streams share contract that plain VLA does not. Each makes future substrate action-facing, either by training future and action prediction together or by using separate action module that consumes the predicted future. We name this family World Action Models (WAMs), following recent usage of this terminology, and make its definition precise in Section 4. This label has spread faster than the understanding behind it. Works that call themselves World Action Models arrive from the video-generation, robot-learning, and language-model communities, and two of them can agree on the name while sharing almost no implementation details. Two others can avoid the name entirely and yet build the same thing. Underneath the disagreement lies common interface question: what predicted future is retained for action, and where along the path is the action decoded. Each answer buys predictive richness at price in compute, memory, and latency inside control loop, which is why the strongest WAMs tend to dream less of the future while still acting on what they need. This survey organizes the resulting works so that any new method can be placed and compared, and it does so from two complementary viewpoints. The first is design-philosophy-level taxonomy, presented in Section 3, that categorizes works by where action is decoded along the inference path and sorts every WAM into exactly one of three families: Render-and-Decode, which carries generation to pixels, Latent-Only, which stops at intermediate representations, and Video-Generation-Free, which is built without the video generator. The second viewpoint, developed in Section 4, is component-level anatomy. It places each WAM on four axes, the predictive substrate that represents the future available to action, the architectural backbone that produces the prediction, the action coupling that joins prediction to control, and the deployment regime the model is built for. unified notation, made precise in that section, expresses every WAM as 4-tuple over these axes. Across both viewpoints we cover the works in our census, listed in Tables 1 and 2, the first holding the video-generation-based families of Render-and-Decode and Latent-Only and the second the Video-Generation-Free family that drops the video backbone. The two viewpoints are complementary, since the philosophy fixes what model is required to generate while the 4-tuple fixes how it is built. Organizing the design space is only half of what this survey provides. The other half is critical account of what these models must do once they are deployed in embodied settings. We examine five properties that embodiment demands, treated in Section 5. WAM must be interactable, accepting control signals during generation rather than only at the start. It must be causal, never letting the future leak into the action executed now. It must be persistent, holding long-horizon predictions together as the robot acts and replans. It must be physically plausible, predicting futures that the embodiment can actually realize. And it must generalize, keeping the same predictive-action contract useful when tasks, objects, scenes, cameras, and embodiments change. For each property we examine how existing methods meet or miss it, and we track what their solutions cost in compute, memory, data, and the effort to evaluate them. The remainder of this survey is organized as follows. From World Models to World Action Models (Section 2). The discussion separates VLAs, broad world models, video generation models, video world models, and WAMs, then defines the action-facing future contract that turns world model into WAM. Three Design Philosophies of World Action Models (Section 3). The philosophy-level taxonomy places every WAM considered here into Render-and-Decode, Latent-Only, or Video-Generation-Free according to what each method is required to generate. What Makes World Action Model (Section 4). The formal anatomy introduces the unified notation and 3 the four axes: predictive substrate, architectural backbone, action coupling, and deployment regime. It then records each WAM as 4-tuple in Tables 1 and 2. Core Properties of World Action Models (Section 5). The property discussion examines interactability, causality, persistence, physical plausibility, and generalization, together with how existing methods address each requirement. Data and Evaluation (Section 6). The section organizes the data sources that fuel WAM training, from teleoperation and portable human demonstrations to simulation and internet-scale egocentric video, alongside the evaluation practices the field is settling on. Open Challenges (Section 7). The final discussion identifies the open problems that the four-axis decomposition brings into view, grouped by where they arise in the WAM stack. Throughout, one observation persists through the survey. Every design choice in WAM carries practical consequence for compute, memory, and latency inside control loop. The interesting question is rarely how much WAM costs on its own. It is how that cost trades against the properties embodiment imposes. Viewed over time, the field has moved toward generating less of the future while holding on to what control requires.

2.1 Vision-Language-Action Models: Policies from Vision-Language Pretraining
Consider an embodied agent that observes o, receives an optional language instruction l, and must choose an
action a. Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models became influential because they showed that pretrained
vision-language and language backbones could be grounded into this action channel. In its simplest form, a
VLA learns
sections.content: LVLA(θ) = E(o,l,a)[ log pθ(a o, l)] . RT-2 [12] established the web-to-robot transfer pattern by co-fine-tuning VLM to output action tokens. OpenVLA [78] made the recipe open and scalable across robot data mixtures, while the π series [11, 71, 72] moved the action side toward continuous flow matching and broader embodiment coverage. These methods are the closest neighbors of WAMs because they already bind vision, language, and action in one action-producing policy. (1) The VLA objective also states the boundary. Equation 1 does not require the model to predict what will be observed after the action is executed. direct VLA can inherit semantic and spatial priors from pretrained VLM, yet still act from the present observation alone. It becomes WAM only when predicted future observation helps produce, choose, or check the action.

sections.title: 2.2 World Models: Predictive Dynamics Beyond Video
sections.content: A world model asks the complementary question. Rather than predicting the action directly, it predicts what future observation should follow from the current observation and an intervention. recent robot-learning survey frames world models as predictive representations of how environments evolve under actions, with uses in policy learning, planning, simulation, evaluation, and data generation [61]. For the present survey, the minimal form is LWM(θ) = E(o,l,a,o)[ log pθ(o o, a, l)] , where is written as an observation for clarity. The symbol is deliberately broad in this section. Depending on the method, it may be rendered pixels, hidden feature, geometric state, an affordance map, an audio cue, symbolic state, or token-level description. Section 4 later separates these cases. In latent model-based reinforcement learning, PlaNet [55], DreamerV3 [57], TransDreamer [18], and Dreamer 4 [58] instantiate this idea with compact dynamics states used for imagination and planning. V-JEPA [7] and V-JEPA 2 [4] show the same predictive idea in feature space, while iVideoGPT [174], RoboDreamer [210], EnerVerse [66], and InteractiveWorldSimulator [166] express it through token or video prediction. (2) 4 Figure 1 Definition of World Action Model. direct VLA predicts action from the present context, and world model predicts future observation. WAM requires that future to stay in the action path, either through predict-then-act cascades, action-scoring rollouts, or joint future-action prediction. Video generation is one visible instance of this broader idea, not its definition. video generation model learns LVGM(θ) = E(r,o)[ log pθ(o r)] , where is prompt, image, observation, or other conditioning signal. When the conditioning includes the agents action and the output is future visual observation, the same family becomes video world model: (3) LVWM(θ) = E(o,l,a,o)[ log pθ(o o, a, l)] . (4) Internet-scale video generators such as Wan, CogVideoX, Cosmos, NOVA, Sora, Latte, AnimateDiff, and VideoPoet [26, 53, 82, 114, 128, 130, 131, 156, 185] made this branch newly influential because they supplied transferable visual dynamics priors. The rise of WAMs follows from this broader world-model success. Video world models gave the field high-capacity source of predictive structure, while feature, geometric, audio, symbolic, and token predictors showed that the useful future need not always be rendered video.

sections.title: 2.3 World Action Models: Making the Future Action-Facing
sections.content: A World Action Model begins when the predicted future observation becomes part of how the action is obtained. The historically early form is cascade: pΘ(o, o, l) = pθ(o o, l) qψ(a o, o, l). (5) The action module qψ may be an inverse-dynamics model, pose tracker, trajectory optimizer, planner, or separately trained policy. Presenting this cascaded form first is historically defensible. Early WAMs such as UniPi [29], VLP [30], and AVDC [81] generated or scored future visual trajectories before recovering executable actions with separate module. Later cascaded WAMs keep the same logic while changing what looks like, as in video-to-trajectory, flow-to-policy, trace-to-control, point-track-to-policy, or future-audio-to-policy pipelines [28, 70, 73, 86, 87, 104, 108, 133, 194]. These works remain WAMs because the predicted future is used to obtain the action, even when the predictor and actor are trained separately. second form proposes an action first and then predicts its consequence: pΘ(o, o, l) = qψ(a o, l) pθ(o o, a, l). (6) 5 This factorization covers candidate-action scoring and planning, where proposed action is evaluated through It is still WAM when the predicted consequence decides which action is its predicted consequence. executed [2, 33, 68, 142, 152]. The same idea can also live inside one trained model: LWAM(θ) = E(o,l,o,a)[ log pθ(o, o, l)] . (7) Here one backbone or tightly coupled set of experts predicts the future observation and the action together. GR-1 and GR-2 [16, 172] helped establish this shared video-action direction with autoregressive image and action tokens, while PAD [52], UWM [213], WorldVLA [14], DreamZero [188], AIM [32], and newer joint designs [92, 141, 209] instantiate the same idea through diffusion, autoregression, or hybrid backbones. Feature-tap methods such as Fast-WAM [193] show that the future branch need not be fully rendered at inference as long as the action path still uses representation shaped by future prediction. The WAM definition is therefore direct and is summarized in Figure 1. VLA models p(a o, l) without needing an explicit future. world model models p(o o, a, l) or p(o o, l) without necessarily choosing the action. WAM links the two: its predicted future observation helps produce the action, score the action, or train the action path inside one model. This definition uses as Section 2 notational simplification. Section 4 instantiates that simplification as different predictive substrates and expands the equations into horizon-level notation. direct VLA with an auxiliary future loss, simulator used only as an RL environment, or future head discarded before action use does not satisfy the definition.

sections.title: 3 Three Design Philosophies of World Action Models
sections.content: Existing world action models share the WAM definition introduced in Section 2. They differ, however, in where the future predictor meets the action module. Some methods train shared video-action backbone, while others keep world model and separate policy, tracker, inverse-dynamics module, or optimizer in cascade. The training pipeline alone therefore does not expose the main design split. The decisive distinction is visible either in the inference forward pass or in how action supervision is wired during training: action prediction may pass through rendered pixel future, stop inside video backbone before rendering, or avoid the video-generation path altogether. The resulting taxonomy has three mutually exclusive philosophies. Render-and-Decode runs the videogeneration backbone all the way to pixel output before action is obtained. The action module may be trained jointly with the generator, trained separately, or supplied by tracking and trajectory optimization. Latent-Only keeps the video-world-model lineage but stops the inference path before pixel decoding. In many cases this means video-generation trunk whose VAE decoder is bypassed, while in others it means video-trained or video-derived latent predictor whose action-facing future has no decoded-pixel stage. Its action signal comes from intermediate latents, partially denoised features, flow fields, semantic masks, or value maps. Video-Generation-Free removes the video-generation backbone from the predictive path. The predictive component instead produces future representation in the embedding or token space of large language model, vision-language model, joint-embedding-predictive encoder, deterministic regressor over frozen vision foundation model, or non-video diffusion or hybrid backbone over compact non-pixel substrate. The taxonomy therefore tracks the requirement imposed during inference or action-supervision, rather than the training data, backbone family, or action interface alone. This split is separable from the cascaded-versus-joint distinction introduced in Section 2.3. Cascaded-versusjoint asks how prediction and action are arranged inside the model. The philosophy-level taxonomy asks where the action prediction is grounded along the inference path: at the rendered pixel output for Renderand-Decode, at an intermediate latent or feature for Latent-Only, or outside the video-generation path for Video-Generation-Free. Latent-Only WAM can be either cascaded or joint, and the same holds for Render-and-Decode WAM. Figure 2 sketches the inference data flow under each philosophy. Figure 3 adds the chronological dimension that the static taxonomy omits. Early WAMs largely adopt the direct path from video generation to control: generate visual future, then decode action from it. Later Latent-Only methods preserve video-derived prior but move the action signal earlier in the inference path, 6 Figure 2 Three design philosophies of World Action Models. The columns separate the last future representation required before action is decoded: rendered pixel future, an intermediate video-derived latent or feature, or non-video-generation representation. The categorization is exhaustive over the WAM census and separable from the action-coupling and backbone choices treated in Section 4. before pixel decoding. Video-Generation-Free methods appear as more recent alternative, replacing the video generator with predictive supervision in the embedding or token space of LLM, VLM, JEPA, or non-video diffusion or hybrid backbones. Over the same period, the application domain has also broadened. Recent entries now cover tabletop manipulation, dexterous hand control, contact-rich tactile interaction, autonomous driving, and aerial manipulation. They instantiate the same three philosophies under substantially different latency requirements and observation regimes. With this chronological view in place, the remainder of this section explains how each philosophy instantiates this design choice across the WAM census. Section 4 then gives the component-level anatomy behind these choices through substrate, backbone family, action coupling, and deployment regime.

sections.title: 3.1 Render-and-Decode World Action Models
sections.content: Render-and-Decode is the most direct path from video generation to action. Its premise is that rendered future is worth producing inside the inference-time control loop because it preserves the full visual prior learned by the video backbone. By running that backbone all the way to pixel output, these methods expose appearance, motion, contact, and scene dynamics to the actor in an inspectable form. The inference pattern is simple: condition video generator on observation history and instruction, produce future video, and decode the next action from that rendered future. The output may be raw RGB, multi-view or 4D RGB-D stream, or sequence of visual tokens that decode back to images, while the action decoder may be inverse dynamics, 6-DoF tracking, dense correspondences, or dedicated action head. This premise gives Render-and-Decode its conceptual clarity, but also makes visual synthesis part of the policys latency budget. The founding branch treats rendered video itself as the plan. UniPi [29] establishes the template with textconditioned video followed by inverse dynamics, VLP [30] adds search over language-level plans, and AVDC [81] shows that dense correspondences can replace action labels. The same interface then moves from simulated or task video to demonstrations with different sources of embodiment evidence. Dreamitate [99] tracks generated stereo video, Gen2Act [9] conditions policy learning on generated human motion, This&That [157] adds gesture coordinates, and GR-MG [89] turns progress-aware goal image into manipulation condition. Later variants refine how the rendered plan becomes executable. CreFlow [125] post-trains the video-as-policy generator with violation masks from temporal-logic monitor, while MoLA [96] maps frozen SVD rollout 7 Figure 3 Chronological stream of representative WAMs grouped by design philosophy. The timeline uses coarse bins for 2023, 2024, and 2025 H1, then finer bins for the denser late-2025 and early-2026 period through May. Each date marker attaches compact vertical stack to the curve, alternating above and below the stream. Dense periods retain early or central verified WAMs so labels remain legible. Stacks above the curve place the widest block nearest the stream, while stacks below the curve begin with the narrowest block. Render-and-Decode appears earliest, Latent-Only emerges as the field starts to drop pixel decoding from the control path, and Video-Generation-Free entries appear as LLM, VLM, JEPA, and non-video diffusion or hybrid backbones begin to carry the predictive component without video-generation core. into semantic, depth, and flow latent-action codebooks before action decoding. The shared lesson is that the rendered future can be common planning currency even when the recovery module changes. Modular Render-and-Decode WAMs make that separation explicit. DreamGen [73] uses generated robot futures to obtain pseudo-actions before downstream policy training, so the coupling is offline rather than inference-time imagination. 4DGen [108], RIGVid [133], TC-IDM [119], and GraspDreamer [147] keep the world model and action recovery separate through pose tracking, VLM filtering, tool-centric point-cloud conversion, or human-grasp retargeting. VERA [94] makes the split even sharper by leaving the video planner action-free and training Jacobian inverse-dynamics translator. These works are useful because the world model can be reused across embodiments, but the downstream module must absorb the gap between visual plausibility and robot executability. related modular pattern inserts geometric or search structure between the generated future and the action. NovaFlow [87] and Dream2Flow [28] distill generated videos into 3D object flow, while EmboAlign [195] filters rollouts with compositional constraints before trajectory optimization. NovaPlan [38] places generated video inside closed-loop manipulation planner, 3D-ALP [142] uses 3D world model as rollout oracle during test-time search, and AeroPlace-Flow [120] grounds task-complete image into object flow for aerial placement. The pixel future remains the first prediction, but control increasingly depends on the structured object recovered from it. Joint Render-and-Decode WAMs move action prediction inside the video backbone rather than attaching separate decoder afterwards. GR-1 [172] is the anchor for this line, because future image tokens and action tokens are learned in one autoregressive stream. GR-2 [16] scales the same video-language-action recipe to larger pretraining and robot-trajectory mixture. WorldVLA [14] adds masking to reduce action leakage while still predicting future VQ images, RynnVLA-002 [13] refines actions through future image-state prediction, PhysGen [144] adapts continuous autoregressive video backbone, and DriveWAM [141] interleaves driving 8 video latents with ego-action tokens on shared DiT. This group changes the coupling pattern, but the action token is still grounded by pixel-decodable future. Diffusion and hybrid variants make the same joint target less sequential. PAD [52] jointly denoises future images and actions in one DiT. UD-VLA [19], CoVAR [183], and VideoVLA [140] vary the bridge through discrete diffusion, cross-stream attention, or video-generator co-training. Motus [10] is placed here by its joint-prediction path, where sparse future video and actions are denoised together. Its scheduler can switch to action-only or inverse-dynamics use cases, but those shortcuts do not change the category assignment. These works keep the action and the future in the same generative state, but make the scheduling less rigid than autoregression. Other joint variants expand what the generated state contains. ActionImages [203] encodes actions as multi-view RGB heatmaps so the observation and action videos share generated channel grid. DriveVA [106] applies flow matching to driving video latents and action tokens, HarmoWAM [36] sends rendered frames and latent features to different action experts, and NoiseGate [67] learns per-latent denoising schedules around frozen joint backbone. The family therefore grows through stronger coupling, not through removing the rendering obligation. Other Render-and-Decode methods change what is rendered so that the future carries more task structure than RGB alone. TesserAct [202] generates RGB-D-Normal 4D video, MVISTA-4D [160] infers arbitrary-view RGB-D through test-time latent optimization, and X-WAM [51] combines multi-view RGB-D prediction with explicit 3D reconstruction. Dex4D [83] lifts generated frames into 3D object point tracks for dexterous control. DriveDreamer-Policy [212] threads depth, video, and action through modular experts, while MV-VDP [90] diffuses future multi-view RGB together with end-effector heatmap videos. These methods keep the visual future, but they make geometry or affordance structure easier for the action side to extract. parallel sparse-future line keeps the output pixel-grounded while reducing how much video must be produced. RoboEnvision [184] uses instruction-aligned keyframes as long-horizon plans. CoT-VLA [201] is the influential VLA-side example, because it generates visual chain-of-thought before action prediction rather than relying on hidden language reasoning alone. pi0.7 [72] follows the same pixel-grounded logic: BAGEL-class generator refreshes multi-view subgoal images asynchronously, and PaliGemma action expert consumes the latest available goals. BagelVLA [63] interleaves subtask language, future keyframes, and action chunks, while SWEET [143] feeds sparse edited keyframes to goal-conditioned diffusion policy. Here the design aim is not to avoid pixels, but to render only the visual milestones that the actor can use. Foundation-scale Render-and-Decode WAMs focus on whether large video prior can be made responsive enough for closed-loop control. DreamZero [188] is the anchor: it turns Wan-class autoregressive video diffusion backbone into an online video-action policy, uses chunked prediction with observation replacement in the KV cache, and shows that large rendered-future WAM can stay inside real control loop. SayDream-Act [48] follows the same practicality question from the distillation side, compressing Cosmos-style imagination into few-step generator before action fusion. Other foundation video planners keep the rendered future as planning object and differ mainly in how they ground it. Vidar [37], LVP [17], and Veo-Act [199] use masked inverse dynamics, Wan-style image-to-video generation, or precision VLA executor. VAG [85] and F1 [111] attach action experts to generated or pooled foresight, while EVA [162] aligns the video planner with an inverse-dynamics reward so the rendered future stays executable. The newest foundation-scale variants spend the video prior more selectively. CKT-WAM [75] transfers context between heterogeneous WAMs, DreamAvoid [33] invokes video imagination only on critical phases, and Pelican-Unify 1.0 [198] links VLM reasoning to Wan-style future generator. This group keeps the rendered future available when it carries task information, while shifting routine action selection toward cheaper context transfer, critical-phase triggering, or grounded VLM guidance. The defining limitation of Render-and-Decode is the price of producing pixels. Each prediction step pays for the full denoising or autoregression schedule, even though the actor rarely consumes the rendered output in its entirety. Moreover, visual-quality metrics inherited from video generation only weakly predict downstream task success. This practical tension links the category closely to the open-loop and chunked closed-loop regimes unpacked in Section 4.5.

sections.title: 3.2 Latent-Only World Action Models
sections.content: The Latent-Only philosophy keeps the video-world-model prior of Render-and-Decode but removes pixel decoding from the inference-time control path. Its motivation is to retain temporal and physical structure learned from video while reducing inference cost. The action signal is decoded directly from latent, an intermediate denoising feature, flow field, semantic mask, value map, or another video-derived feature future, each of which is cheaper to produce than fully rendered video. The trade-off is that these methods give up the pixel-space supervision and visual-quality metrics that make Render-and-Decode easy to inspect. The designs differ mainly in where they intercept the predictive path: some consume the models own video latent, some tap an intermediate denoising state, some predict structured substrate such as object flow or masks, and some inherit joint video-action backbone but disable pixel decoding at inference. In all cases, the key move is to keep video-shaped dynamic prior while refusing to pay for the final renderer during action selection. The earliest Latent-Only entries make the interception point explicit. ARDuP [65] focuses latent video diffusion on active regions, VPP [62] conditions inverse dynamics on Stable Video Diffusion representations, and VILP [178] recovers actions from time-aligned multi-view latent video. UVA [93] and Video Policy [100] show the two main training patterns: either share video-action latent across heads, or freeze the video generator and train the policy on its intermediate features. Genie Envisioner [102] is the clearest bridge between these patterns. GE-Base remains an instruction-conditioned multi-view video DiT, while GE-Act decodes action chunks from one-step denoised latent cache through flow matching. Act2Goal [208] reuses the same foundation for goal-conditioned transition features, and WAV [91] performs value-guided latent trajectory inference before action decoding. These methods show that the first shortcut is to stop at the representation that action already needs. As video diffusion backbones scale, Latent-Only designs increasingly intercept the denoising trajectory rather than finished latent. mimic-video [132] attaches an action decoder to an intermediate ODE checkpoint of Cosmos-Predict2, and DiT4DiT [113] conditions its action expert on intermediate Cosmos-Predict2.5 features in deterministic step. S-VAM [181] self-distills multi-step denoising into one pass over geometric and semantic features. Fast-WAM [193] and GigaWorld-Policy [186] keep future-video co-training but make full generation optional or unnecessary at inference. LaMP [164] exposes one-step 3D motion hidden state to VLA through gated cross-attention, and VAMPO [42] tunes early-step VPM latents against expert visual dynamics. The practical shift is from pixel synthesis to feature extraction. Another branch predicts structured motion before action decoding. Im2Flow2Act [177] and 3DFlowAction [207] replace appearance with object flow, while 3D-FDP [126] predicts dense 3D flow over query points before second action diffusion model runs. TraceGen [86] predicts 3D traces that inverse kinematics converts into joint commands. This flow-and-trace group compresses the future toward motion rather than texture. The structured-substrate branch then broadens beyond flow. 3PoinTr [70] and RoboFlow4D [104] freeze future point-track or 4D-flow predictors and train downstream policies to consume the predicted motion. MWM [109] keeps video-diffusion architecture but predicts semantic-mask evolution, OmniVTA [206] adds tactile latents for contact-rich control, and EgoExo-WM [152] uses DINOv3 latent regressor under model-predictive control after exo-to-ego data conversion. This branch makes the future closer to contact and task state than rendered frame would be. The clearest Latent-Only inference pattern appears when joint video-action backbone is trained with visual prediction but used through latent action path. UWM [213] is the reference case: independent diffusion timesteps let the method collapse the visual branch at inference while retaining the video-trained representation. CosmosPolicy [79], AdaWorldPolicy [191], and MotuBrain [121] follow the pixel-latent version of the same idea, where future RGB or video latents remain useful during training but the action call at inference does not require fully rendered RGB forecast. LingBot-VA [88] exposes semantic structures before pixel reconstruction, and AIM [32] decodes action through spatial value map rather than directly through future RGB. The design consequence is clear: visual prediction shapes the representation, but action selection does not have to render the visual prediction. Recent Latent-Only WAMs extend the same idea across modalities and memory designs. GigaBrain-0.5M* [158] feeds the policy with future-state latents and value embeddings from Wan2.2 world model. VTAM [192] 10 treats camera views and GelSight tactile input as decoder-bound latent views, while CLWM [27] forecasts future DINOv3 feature maps and replaces growing KV cache with Dual-State Test-Time Training memory. The same inference logic also travels across domains and joint-state designs. DAWN [110] iteratively refines V-JEPA latent world tokens against DiT action hypotheses, JOPAT [49] adds point tracks and visibility to the joint denoising state, and tau0-WM [209] keeps Wan-style video latents in the joint state but decodes action without routine pixel rendering. WALL-WM [92] pushes the same idea into depth-wise cross-attention between matched video blocks and action queries. These examples make Latent-Only design philosophy rather than single substrate choice. newer branch uses latent imagination as an adaptive control signal. FFDC-WAM [161] caches Motus latent video tokens with the proposed action chunk and uses causal attention verifier to decide when full WAM replan is needed. tau0-WM [209] can also reactivate its action-conditioned video simulator only when candidate scoring is useful. This turns imagination into learned controller decision: the policy invokes the heavier future model only when confidence drops. Across this family, the central tension is clear. Video pretraining supplies valuable dynamic prior, but full pixel rendering imposes latency and memory costs that are difficult to justify for every control step. Latent-Only methods preserve the prior while bypassing the renderer, enabling several members of the family to reach real-time inference at the cost of reduced direct visual interpretability.

sections.title:
3.3 Video-Generation-Free World Action Models
The Video-Generation-Free philosophy removes the video-generation backbone altogether. Here video-
generation-free refers strictly to the absence of a pixel-level video backbone in the predictive path. The
LLM, VLM, JEPA, and flow-matching components below remain generative models in the broader sense.
Its motivation is to avoid the training and inference cost of video generation while retaining predictive
supervision in a more compact representational space. These methods instead leverage large language
models, vision-language models, joint-embedding-predictive encoders, deterministic regressors over frozen
vision foundation models, or non-video diffusion and hybrid backbones over a compact non-pixel substrate.
The dominant pattern attaches a lightweight action expert to a VLM, learns a latent-action vocabulary
that bridges actionless video and robot control, or supervises a policy with an auxiliary feature-prediction
loss in a vision-foundation embedding space. The family is listed in Table 2, where it complements the
Render-and-Decode and Latent-Only entries in Table 1.
sections.content: The feature-prediction line replaces pixel prediction with teacher-target or foundation-feature prediction. FLARE [204] is the anchor: additional policy tokens are aligned to future-observation embeddings from frozen teacher, and the action expert consumes those predicted future tokens at inference. FRAPPE [200] extends the idea by aligning generalist policy to future representations from multiple vision foundation models, while DexWM [44] applies deterministic future-feature regression to dexterous hand-object interaction. LDA-1B [112] moves the same principle into Qwen3-VL stack by jointly learning dynamics, policy, and forecasting in structured DINO latent. These works keep the predictive training signal, but place it in an embedding space whose quality is judged through action rather than photorealism. Another Video-Generation-Free line treats the future as learned token or latent transition. DUST [171] augments an Eagle-2 VLM with dual-stream diffusion head that predicts future observation embedding tokens and an action together without video-generation foundation. ALAM [149] learns latent transitions from action-free video and regularizes them with composition and reversal consistency before flow-matching action expert consumes the codes. The shared move is to keep future object in the inference path while choosing representation that is cheaper than video. Structured non-pixel futures give the family its second anchor. PointWorld [68] predicts action-conditioned 3D point flow through deterministic Point Transformer and plans with MPPI over the compact future. PALM [107] structures foresight as affordance maps plus progress, while HiF-VLA [103] uses compact motionvector foresight tokens and ICLR-VR [124] predicts gripper-keypoint polyline before each action chunk. These works replace video with geometry, affordance, or trace-like variables that are closer to the control interface. 11 The remaining generation-free variants broaden the same principle across modalities and feedback. AudioWM [194] predicts future Mel latents that condition robot policy when contact sound carries task evidence. DDP [64] switches to latent imagination when real-imagination gap indicates an out-of-distribution event, and Feedback-WM [2] treats learned latent transition model as an observer that corrects diffusion-policy denoising online. Together, these methods show what is gained by dropping the video generator entirely, and what must be replaced by tokens, features, audio latents, affordances, or geometry.

sections.title: 4 What Makes a World Action Model
sections.content: The term world action model now spans works built in different communities, with different vocabularies, and on top of different backbones. paper-by-paper enumeration is insufficient for comparing them. Methods that share the WAM label can differ in nearly every implementation detail, whereas closely related methods may use entirely different terminology. This section takes different route. It treats WAMs as instances of common mathematical object: conditional joint distribution over future predictions and future actions. Under this view, the main design choices specify what future is represented, how actions are coupled to that future, what model family produces the prediction, and how the resulting model is invoked during control. We refer to these choices as the predictive substrate, action coupling, architectural backbone, and deployment regime, respectively. Figure 4 previews how the four ingredients combine. Section 4.1 introduces the shared notation. Sections 4.2 through 4.5 then walk through the four ingredients one by one. They sort existing methods along the choices each ingredient offers and note the practical trade-off that each choice carries.

sections.title: 4.1 A Unified Notation for World Action Models
sections.content: At the right level of generality, WAMs can be described by common abstract object. The object is parameterized conditional joint distribution over window of future predictions and window of future actions: pΘ( st+1:t+H , at:t+H1 ot, a<t, ) . Here ot = (o0, . . . , ot) is the observation history, with each oi an observation, such as frame, video chunk, or multimodal sensory packet, a<t = (a0, . . . , at1) is the past action history with ai A, and is task instruction in language, goal image, or another auxiliary modality. The model predicts future trajectory st+1:t+H of length in chosen substrate space together with future action chunk at:t+H1 of the same length. The parameter collection Θ may be one shared backbone or retained world-model module paired with an action module. To keep later equations compact, we abbreviate the full conditioning context as (8) ( ot, a<t, ), (9) and write Equation 8 as pΘ(st+1:t+H , at:t+H1 c) whenever no ambiguity arises. The substrate space is the space of the future representation that the WAM exposes to action production or action evaluation. We write it as the image of an interface encoder ϕ : S, but ϕ should not be taken as the generators internal implementation. pixel-grounded WAM exposes decoded future frames, or the VAE / VQ latent grid that the generator decodes to clip, even if the action side never invokes the pixel decode at inference. feature WAM exposes learned hidden state or token block with no decodable visual 12 Figure 4 The anatomy of world action model. Any existing WAM can be specified by four separable but interacting choices: what it predicts (Section 4.2), how action is coupled to that prediction (Section 4.3), the function family that produces the prediction (Section 4.4), and the deployment regime it is intended for (Section 4.5). Two methods with the same labels but different choices on any one axis behave very differently in practice. interpretation, whether it is frozen self-supervised teachers target, feature tap of generative trunk, or VLM future-token block. geometric-primitive WAM exposes structured object whose dimensions are physical coordinates, such as flow, point clouds, depth, pose, or motion vectors. An affordance-map WAM exposes task-relevant label or score map, such as value, affordance, or end-effector heatmap. The symbol pΘ in Equation 8 is placeholder for an abstract joint, not necessarily one monolithic network. Section 4.4 lists the five concrete families that realize its future-prediction factor in practice, each with its own parameterization. Until that point, pΘ should be treated as the action-facing future contract that every WAM must satisfy. The four ingredients of this section are choices about how Equation 8 is realized in practice. (i) Predictive substrate. choice of action-facing future representation, and therefore of the space in which st+1:t+H lives. Section 4.2. (ii) Action coupling. choice of factorization of the joint in Equation 8. Three top-level factorization families appear across existing WAMs and return in Section 4.3: action-conditioned rollout : (cid:40) qψ(at:t+H1 c) pθ(st+1:t+H c, at:t+H1) , (cid:81)H1 k=0 qψ(at+k hk, c) pθ(st+k+1 hk, at+k, c) , chunk-level, step-wise, joint generation : pθ(st+1:t+H , at:t+H1 c) , post-prediction head : pθ(st+1:t+H c) qψ(at:t+H1 st+1:t+H , c) . (10) (11) (12) where hk = (st+1:t+k, at:t+k1) in the step-wise submode. Action-conditioned rollout composes an action source with world predictor, either by fixing an action chunk before prediction or by updating the action after each predicted step. Joint generation produces the future substrate and the future action chunk from one coupled generative model pθ(st+1:t+H , at:t+H1 c), where θ may include shared trunk as well as modality-specific heads or experts The post-prediction head factorization predicts the substrate first and then decodes action from it with smaller actor qψ, which is often tracker, inverse-dynamics model, optimizer, planner, or policy that can be trained separately from θ. All three families carry the past action history through the abbreviation c, so the conditioning sides remain consistent with Equation 8. (iii) Architectural backbone. choice of function family that realizes the future-prediction factor of pΘ, namely an iterative denoising network, an autoregressive decoder, joint-embedding predictor, hybrid that combines two of the above, or large language or vision-language model with an attached action decoder. Each family carries its own parameterization, which we introduce only when the family is treated. Section 4.4. (iv) Deployment regime. choice of when the WAM is invoked and over what window. Open-loop rollout invokes the WAM once with and specifies the whole window before execution. Chunked closed-loop invokes the WAM every control steps with = and replans after each chunk is executed. Single-step closed-loop invokes the WAM at every control step with = 1. Interactive operation typically reuses trained model θ and extends indefinitely while carrying state across calls through key-value cache or persistent latent. Section 4.5. This decomposition is useful not because the four axes are separable, but because they separate different questions about WAM. The substrate asks what kind of future variable is represented, the backbone asks how that variable is predicted, the coupling asks how actions enter the prediction or are recovered from it, and the deployment regime asks how the resulting model is invoked inside control loop. In practice, the substrate and backbone are usually fixed by training, while deployment and some wrapper-level coupling choices can be adjusted at inference. Changing the coupling family itself, however, often requires an additional action head, planner, or fine-tuning stage. We use these four questions as the organizing lens for the subsections below. Tables 1 and 2 place every WAM in our census as 4-tuple over these choices, and the subsections that follow walk through the choices one by one and anchor each one in the same equation.

sections.title:
4.2 Predictive Substrate: Where WAMs Dream
The first choice in Equation 8 is where the future variable st+1:t+H lives. We call this space the predictive
substrate. The substrate is not determined by the backbone alone, nor by the last tensor consumed by the
action head. For example, a video-diffusion model may denoise a VAE grid and skip pixel decoding during
control. As long as a fixed decoder maps that grid back to video, the predicted future remains pixel-grounded.
Conversely, a policy may consume an intermediate state from a video-trained trunk, but that state is a
feature substrate if it has no fixed observation decoder. In this section, we therefore classify a WAM by the
representation in which it forms the future used for action prediction or evaluation.
sections.content: We use four substrate categories. Pixel-grounded substrates represent future observations, either as decoded RGB, RGB-D, multi-view video, or as VAE / VQ latents with fixed video decoder. Acoustic observation latents are rare in the current literature, so we treat them as multimodal observation-latent edge cases rather than as separate category. Feature substrates represent learned states, teacher embeddings, or VLM token blocks without fixed observation decoder. Geometric substrates represent physical structure or motion, such as optical flow, point tracks, depth, pose, motion vectors, or polylines. Affordance substrates represent task-specific maps or score fields, such as value maps, contact maps, semantic masks, or heatmaps. Some WAMs predict more than one kind of future in the same forward process. The tables mark these joint cases with . 4.2.1 Pixel-grounded substrates The pixel-grounded category is the direct descendant of video generation. Its decoded form is an observation tensor, st+1:t+H RHCoHpxWpx , where Co is the observation channel count. decoder-bound latent variant uses the same category when is VAE, VQ, or 3D-VAE code whose fixed decoder maps it back to video. The model may act before that decoder is called, but the future still lives in an observation coordinate frame. This criterion separates decoder-bound observation latents from feature latents below. (13) Decoded observation futures. Early WAMs take the direct route: generate future image or video, then decode action from it. UniPi [29] establishes this pattern with text-conditioned video plus inverse dynamics, while AVDC [81] shows that dense correspondences from the decoded video can replace action labels. The same substrate category covers cascaded designs when the action module is separate. DreamGen [73] uses decoded 14 robot futures to obtain pseudo-actions before policy training, RIGVid [133] retargets tracked generated demonstrations, and VERA [94] lets Jacobian inverse-dynamics model act from generated visual lookahead. Later decoded-observation methods keep the same substrate but broaden the content of the forecast. Vidar [37], LVP [17], and EVA [162] use stronger Wan-class planners, while Veo-Act [199] uses precision VLA executor on top of generated visual plan. These wrappers still belong to the pixel-grounded category when geometry is recovered after the visual future has been produced. Pixel-decodable latent futures. The second route keeps the future in the pixel-decodable latent space of video generator. GR-1 [172] and GR-2 [16] predict VQ image tokens and action tokens in one autoregressive stream. PAD [52], UWM [213], WorldVLA [14], VideoVLA [140], DriveVA [106], and τ0-WM [209] instead couple action to diffusion or flow-matching trajectory over VAE video latents. Hybrid entries such as F1 [111], Motus [10], Pelican-Unify 1.0 [198], NoiseGate [67], and WALL-WM [92] keep the same substrate while changing the routing between video and action streams. CKT-WAM [75] and FFDC-WAM [161] add teacher context or verifier around that substrate, but the action-facing future remains the student or Motus pixel-decodable visual forecast. These works are not feature-substrate WAMs under the present criterion because the future is still compressed observation under fixed observation decoder. Sparse, multimodal, and joint observation futures. The pixel-grounded category also covers reduced, multichannel, and rare non-visual observation displays. SWEET [143] shrinks the forecast to task-critical keyframes. Audio-WM [194] predicts future Mel latents that condition robot policy, and it is grouped here as an acoustic observation-latent edge case because it follows the same decoder-bound criterion without changing the category name. DriveDreamer-Policy [212] predicts decoded video together with depth, while MV-VDP [90] and ActionImages [203] predict RGB together with Gaussian end-effector heatmaps. OmniVTA [206] is also an observation-future case rather than geometric one, because its future is pair of visual and tactile decoder-bound latents with fixed decoders, not coordinate tensor. HarmoWAM [36] is dual observation case because its Wan backbone produces both rendered frames and pixel-decodable latent features that feed different action experts. The common trade-off is clear: observation futures preserve appearance, contact traces, and broad video priors, but they pay for detail that many action decisions do not need. 4.2.2 Feature substrates The feature category covers futures that are learned states rather than decoder-bound observation states. single sample can be written as st+1:t+H RHNtokdemb , with Ntok tokens per step and embedding dimension demb. The key property is absence of fixed observation decoder. The state may be trained through future prediction, but it is interpreted only by the action pathway or by an embedding loss. (14) Encoder-only and feature-tap futures. This variant keeps the future-trained backbone but routes action through an internal state. Fast-WAM [193] is the clean encoder-only case: it keeps video co-training but masks the test-time future branch, so action is predicted from hidden state shaped by future prediction. VPP [62], Genie Envisioner [102], VidMan [170], mimic-video [132], VAG [85], and WAV [91] keep more of the generative trajectory but decode action from latent or intermediate denoising states. LaMP [164] is the VLM-stack version of the same shortcut, exposing motion-expert hidden state through gated cross-attention before the action expert fires. The boundary is that an auxiliary hidden state is not enough to make the substrate feature future when the inference-time action-facing future is still pixel-decodable visual forecast. Teacher-target futures. second feature route predicts the embedding of frozen vision encoder, multimodal encoder, or policy/expert latent state. FLARE [204] aligns policy tokens to future-observation embeddings, and LDA-1B [112] learns dynamics, policy, and forecasting in structured DINO feature space on Qwen3-VL backbone. DAWN [110], CLWM [27], EgoExo-WM [152], DexWM [44], DDP [64], Feedback-WM [2], and FRAPPE [200] all keep the future in learned feature space rather than in decoder-bound observation latent. The benefit is compact prediction and strong semantic invariance. The cost is that the substrate has no direct visual-fidelity metric. VLM-token futures. The third feature route represents the future as tokens in VLM vocabulary. CoTVLA [201] predicts visual chain-of-thought tokens before action generation. DUST [171] jointly diffuses next-state observation token and an action token, and ALAM [149] interleaves view-specific latent-transition tokens with action tokens under structured attention mask. These methods use tokens as future carriers, not merely as action discretisation. The feature category is therefore broad, but its practical boundary is whether the predicted future lacks fixed observation decoder and is not physical coordinate tensor or task map. 4.2.3 Geometric primitive substrates The third category exposes structured object whose dimensions are physical coordinates. common instance is dense optical flow, for which ϕ is an off-the-shelf flow operator and single sample is st+1:t+H RH2HpxWpx , (15) namely two channels carrying the (u, v) displacement field. Variants replace the flow field with Nkp tracked keypoints in RHNkp3, depth tensor, 6-DoF pose stream, sparse waypoint polyline, or macroblock motion-vector grid. method belongs here when geometry is the predicted future representation, not when tracker merely measures geometry after pixel-grounded future has already been chosen. Flow and point-motion futures. Im2Flow2Act [177] and 3DFlowAction [207] motivate the category by replacing full appearance with object motion. NovaFlow [87] and Dream2Flow [28] take the cascaded route by extracting 3D object flow from generated videos before planning. TraceGen [86] predicts 3D traces, while 3PoinTr [70] and RoboFlow4D [104] feed separately predicted point tracks or flow into downstream policies. 3D-FDP [126], PointWorld [68], and Dex4D [83] extend this idea from dense image flow to sparse or scene-level 3D point trajectories. The common payoff is future that is closer to control than RGB. Structured physical futures. TesserAct [202] reconstructs 4D point cloud as the control-facing representation. HiF-VLA [103] uses MPEG-4 motion vectors as compact future-motion field, and ICLR-VR [124] reduces the future to five-point gripper polyline. JOPAT [49] is one prominent joint case because it denoises pixel-decodable visual future together with point tracks and visibility. Geometric futures are cheaper and often easier to supervise than pixel-grounded futures, but they lose appearance cues that are irrelevant to motion yet useful for semantic grounding. The structured tensor is far smaller than the pixel tensor at the same spatial resolution, and it can often be trained stably on less data. The trade-off is that these predictions are most reliable when motion, contact, or geometry carries the task-relevant information. They are less suitable when appearance or semantics is essential. 4.2.4 Affordance map substrates The fourth category exposes task-relevant label or score map. single sample is st+1:t+H RHCHpxWpx , (16) with channels carrying value, affordance, contact likelihood, segmentation, or progress information. The substrate is not an observation, feature, or physical coordinate field. It is spatial answer to task-specific question. Value, mask, and affordance futures. AIM [32] inserts spatial value map between future visual dynamics and action, with intent-causal attention forcing action to pass through that value representation. PALM [107] predicts bundle of future affordance maps, including object masks, contact likelihood, placement candidates, and motion regions. MWM [109] predicts semantic-mask dynamics rather than RGB, so its future is label map rather than generic hidden state. ActionImages [203] and MV-VDP [90] are joint pixel-grounded-andaffordance cases because they predict RGB together with Gaussian end-effector heatmaps. Affordance futures are therefore more task-specific than observation or feature futures. They are compact and often directly useful for manipulation, but their labels must be defined for the task at hand. 4.2.5 Substrate and coupling are distinct axes The four categories classify the space of the future variable s, while Section 4.3 classifies how and are tied together. The distinction matters. Fast-WAM [193] is feature-substrate and encoder-only because its future Figure 5 The four predictive-substrate categories of Section 4.2, classified by the representational space in which the WAM forms its future. Pixel-grounded substrates include decoded observations and pixel-decodable latents, with Audio-WM treated as rare acoustic observation-latent edge case (14, 29, 172, 194, 209). Feature substrates include encoderonly, feature-tap, teacher-target, and VLM-token futures with no fixed observation decoder (62, 110, 149, 193, 204). Geometric substrates expose flow, point clouds, depth, polylines, or motion vectors (68, 103, 202). Affordance substrates expose value maps, masks, affordances, or heatmaps (32, 107, 109). Joint cells use the separator when one forward pass predicts futures in two categories. branch is masked at inference and the action expert uses hidden state. τ0-WM [209] is pixel-groundedsubstrate and joint-denoising because it predicts Wan VAE video latent together with action velocity. mimic-video [132] is feature-substrate and post-prediction-head because the action head taps an intermediate ODE state of frozen video trunk. The substrate names where the WAM forms its future. The coupling axis names how that future reaches action. Figure 5 arranges the categories and representative members on the same panel.

sections.title: 4.3 Action Coupling: How Action Enters and Leaves
sections.content: Action coupling is the structural decision that turns predictive world model into WAM. The factorization families in Equations 10, 11, and 12 pin down the main arrangements. Action-conditioned rollout is composite family: an outer planner, policy, candidate sampler, or teleoperation stream supplies actions through qψ, and an action-conditioned world model predicts their consequences. Joint generation and post-prediction heads are action-producing families, either because the future action chunk is produced by the same joint model or because it is decoded from the produced future by separate head. Within each arrangement, existing methods further differ on how an action is represented and on how many actions are produced per forward pass. Figure 6 sketches the three top-level arrangements and shows representative candidate forms inside the first. Notely, in this section, when we refer to method as non-census, we mean that it is cited for background but is not listed as WAM in Tables 1 and 2. 17 Figure 6 Three common action-coupling families in existing WAMs. The arrangements differ in where the action representation is bound to the substrate prediction, and that binding is what determines latency, controllability, and per-step inference cost. 4.3.1 Action-conditioned rollout Action-conditioned rollout composes an action source with an action-conditioned world predictor. Repeating Equation 10, pθ,ψ(st+1:t+H , at:t+H1 c) = (cid:40) qψ(at:t+H1 c) pθ(st+1:t+H c, at:t+H1) , (cid:81)H1 k=0 qψ(at+k hk, c) pθ(st+k+1 hk, at+k, c) , chunk-level, step-wise, (17) hk = (st+1:t+k, at:t+k1) . The first line is chunk-level rollout. The future action chunk is output by qψ and enters the substrate predictor as conditioning. The second line is step-wise rollout. It alternates action selection with actionconditioned prediction, so qψ can update the next action after each predicted state. Training usually applies the standard substrate-prediction loss of Section 4.4 to pθ, with the proposed action or action chunk added to the conditioning side. The action source may be planner, policy, human command stream, fixed candidate sampler, or, in the step-wise background lineage, diffusion policy queried after each predicted state. In the current census, the WAMs listed in the tables in this family all sit on the chunk-level submode. The step-wise submode is retained to connect the WAM notation to the model-based RL and interactive-prediction lineage discussed below. The chunk-level submode is the natural form when the method scores candidate futures before execution. Cosmos-Transfer1 [129] illustrates nearby non-census case: segmentation, depth, edge, and other spatial control maps condition world generation for data generation, but the method does not use generated futures as an inference-time robot-action selector. PointWorld [68] conditions point-flow dynamics model on proposed sequence of robot point flows from MPPI and scores the predicted scene flow, so action search happens outside the predictor rather than inside the trunk. DreamAvoid [33] samples flow-policy action chunks only at detected critical phases, renders each chunk with DreamDojo, and executes the candidate with the highest predicted progress value. EgoExo-WM [152] conditions DINOv3-L latent predictor on horizon-length UniEgoMotion candidate body-motion sequences, rolls each candidate forward under MPC, and selects the sequence whose predicted latent ends closest to visual goal. 18 Tree-search and feedback-guided variants keep the same family but change the cadence of candidate evaluation. 3D-ALP [142] uses MCTS to propose joint actions, converts each proposal into 3D camera pose through kinematics, and uses 3D-consistent renderer as rollout oracle before executing the selected action. FeedbackWM [2] uses lightweight latent transition model to guide diffusion policy while that policy forms an action chunk, passes the corrected chunk to the retained world-prediction path, and then updates an auxiliary feedback state from the observation after execution. These variants explain why the family should not be split by whether actions arrive as one candidate chunk, tree node, or corrected action sequence. In all cases, actions are supplied to predictor that evaluates their consequences. The step-wise submode remains useful for background and for future WAMs whose action source reacts inside the imagined trajectory. AdaWorld [40] conditions an SVD-style latent-video diffusion predictor at each rollout step, although it is not part of the census. FlowDreamer [50] cascades geometric-primitive flow prediction into diffusion next-frame generator, two-stage variant of the same arrangement. The latent-imagination Dreamer line of PlaNet [55], DreamerV3 [57], Dreamer 4 [58], and TransDreamer [18] supplies the model-based RL ancestor of this design, where planner, actor, critic, or compact dynamics model advances imagined steps depending on the member. The non-WAM antecedent iVideoGPT [174] interleaves observation, action, and reward tokens in an autoregressive stream, while InteractiveWorldSimulator [166] and RoboScape [139] extend interactive prediction with streaming inputs or physics-aware branches. Action-conditioned rollout is therefore composite WAM factorization rather than an action-producing world model by itself. Its advantage is counterfactual control: candidate actions shape the future before selection. Its limitation depends on the submode. Chunk-level rollout can evaluate many candidates in parallel, but it cannot react to control signal that changes inside the predicted window. Step-wise rollout can react after each predicted state, but it loses the parallelism of single generated window. 4.3.2 Joint generation The joint factorization produces substrate and action from single generative process on shared backbone. Repeating Equation 11, pθ(st+1:t+H , at:t+H1 c) . Training is often expressible as, or approximated by, weighted sum of substrate-side generative loss and an action-side regression or classification loss, (18) (19) Ljoint(θ) = Lgen(s) + λ Lact(a), where Lgen is diffusion or autoregression loss on and Lact is flow-matching, regression, or cross-entropy loss on a. Section 4.4 reuses Equation 19 as the canonical training objective of hybrid backbones. Joint diffusion of video and action. The joint-diffusion group differs less in its factorization than in the interface through which the two streams meet. UWM [213], CosmosPolicy [79], VideoVLA [140], DriveVA [106], and DriveWAM [141] place video-side latents and action-side variables in the same denoising or flow-matching state, so one sampled trajectory carries both the imagined future and the action chunk. CoVAR [183], UDVLA [19], GigaWorld-Policy [186], and CKT-WAM [75] keep the same joint target but change the information routing through bridge attention, discrete diffusion, action-centered masking, or teacher-context prefixes. Multisubstrate variants add another future channel without changing the coupling: AIM [32] inserts value map, DriveDreamer-Policy [212] orders depth, video, and action inside one training pass, MV-VDP [90] and ActionImages [203] attach end-effector heatmaps to the visual stream, VTAM [192] adds tactile force proxy, HiF-VLA [103] adds motion-vector foresight tokens, and DUST [171] applies the same idea to VLM-substrate setting. The useful distinction is therefore whether the added channel changes the substrate, not whether the action is still jointly produced. Joint autoregression of frame and action tokens. GR-1 [172] and GR-2 [16] jointly predict next-frame and nextaction tokens autoregressively over discrete codebook. WorldVLA [14] carries the same recipe to Chameleon backbone with an action-chunk attention mask between action and frame tokens. PhysGen [144] adapts the autoregressive NOVA backbone with continuous physical tokens and per-token diffusion detokenizer, so that autoregressive image and action streams share joint training loss in the sense of Equation 19. ICLR-VR [124] serializes state, reasoning, and action tokens into single Llama2-style causal stream under combined next-token loss, with the gripper-keypoint polyline decoded immediately before each action chunk. 19 Mixture-of-experts hybrids that joint-denoise. The hybrid branch uses separate experts or heads so that video and action can share trunk without forcing every layer to treat both variables identically. UVA [93] and PAD [52] are the clean baselines: one shared pass supports both visual prediction and action, and the visual branch can be bypassed when only control is needed. F1 [111], Motus [10], MotuBrain [121], RynnVLA-002 [13], BagelVLA [63], Pelican-Unify 1.0 [198], and ALAM [149] instantiate the mixture pattern with different expert allocations for understanding, video, latent transition, and action streams. second set keeps the same joint target but changes the schedule: DreamZero [188] uses one shared timestep across Wan-style video-action state, X-WAM [51] lets action finish before multi-view video, NoiseGate [67] learns per-latent denoising increments, τ0-WM [209] samples video and action flow times independently, and WALL-WM [92] couples action queries into matched video blocks at every depth. CLWM [27], DAWN [110], JOPAT [49], and DDP [64] show the same coupling on feature, track, or compact latent substrates, while FFDC-WAM [161] keeps the Motus joint rollout and adds verifier that decides when the cached future is still trustworthy. The family is diverse, but the shared claim is narrow: the action and the future substrate are trained as mutually constrained variables rather than as planner output followed by passive decoder. The benefit of the family is one coupled sample or rollout that produces both predictions and strong mutual consistency between them. The trade-off is potential training instability because the generation and action losses can pull the shared representation in different directions. Practitioners therefore often pretrain the substrate head on video and introduce the action head later, sometimes with careful λ schedule in Equation 19. 4.3.3 Post-prediction action head The post-prediction head factorizes into substrate generator and smaller action expert qψ that decodes action from the substrate. Repeating Equation 12, pθ(st+1:t+H , at:t+H1 c) = pθ(st+1:t+H c) qψ(at:t+H1 st+1:t+H , c) , (20) with θ frozen during qψ training in many implementations. This arrangement is common because it lets pretrained predictor stay fixed while the action head changes across embodiments. Video-plan then action-recovery WAMs. UniPi [29] canonical early example: text-conditioned diffusion planner first synthesizes future image trajectory, and task-specific inverse-dynamics module then recovers the low-level actions between generated frames. VLP [30] adds VLM policy and value-guided search over abstract text actions, but the executable controls still come from goal-conditioned policies that consume the synthesized video plan. EVA [162] pairs Wan2.1-14B planner with frozen spatial-softmax IDM and uses the IDM-derived smoothness-and-limits reward to align the planner via GRPO without folding the IDM into the joint denoiser. CreFlow [125] post-trains the Vidar text-image-to-video trunk against an LTL-grounded corrective reflow loss and keeps the Vidar inverse-dynamics expert frozen. These methods are often described as video planning or video world modeling, but under the notation here their action coupling is the post-prediction factorization of Equation 20. Action expert on latent, token, or multimodal substrate. This group keeps the action expert separate, so the key question is what future object qψ consumes. Some members expose task-structured future before action decoding: π0.7 [72] uses BAGEL-style subgoal images as visual targets, CoT-VLA [201] uses visual chain-of-thought tokens, PALM [107] uses affordance maps, and OmniVTA [206] uses visual and tactile latents whose fixed decoders tie them back to observation space for slow-fast contact-rich control. Featuresubstrate members differ mainly in where the future feature comes from. FLARE [204], LDA-1B [112], FRAPPE [200], and GigaBrain-0.5M* [158] rely on future embeddings or value-bearing latent tokens, while VPP [62], VideoPolicy [100], Genie Envisioner [102], LaMP [164], and VAMPO [42] intercept intermediate states of video-trained trunk. The action expert can therefore be small, but its reliability depends on whether the chosen substrate preserves the action-relevant part of the forecast. Action expert decodes structured geometry from generated future. The geometric post-head line uses motion or structure as the intermediate action-facing representation. Im2Flow2Act [177], 3DFlowAction [207], 3DFDP [126], NovaFlow [87], Dream2Flow [28], and AeroPlace-Flow [120] make flow the intermediate future, while TesserAct [202], 4DGen [108], MVISTA-4D [160], TC-IDM [119], and Dex4D [83] recover pose or point structure before control. Earlier decoded-video cascades such as AVDC [81], Dreamitate [99], This&That [157], 20 ARDuP [65], Gen2Act [9], GR-MG [89], and GraspDreamer [147] fit the same factorization even when the extracted geometry is implicit in tracker or learned head. RoboEnvision [184], Vidar [37], LVP [17], Veo-Act [199], Say-Dream-Act [48], NovaPlan [38], and MoLA [96] keep the same post-head order but add stronger video planners, VLM scoring, or modality-specific inverse dynamics. The shared lesson is that generated future can be action-facing even when the final action module is trained separately. Generated future is partly or fully optional at inference. The optional-future pattern trains with substrate generator, then shortens or bypasses that generator when the inference-time action path can use an internal state. VidMan [170] is the clearest representative: it mounts policy head on frozen video-diffusion features rather than waiting for rendered future. mimic-video [132], S-VAM [181], DiT4DiT [113], Fast-WAM [193], and VAG [85] make the same move through ODE checkpoints, distilled foresight states, fixed denoising timesteps, masked future-video tokens, or pooled clean latents. WAV [91], MWM [109], DexWM [44], and SWEET [143] instead keep compact action-facing substrate, such as valued latent rollouts, semantic-mask dynamics, DINO latents, or sparse keyframes. LingBot-VA [88] and HarmoWAM [36] show the mixturebackbone version, where visual dynamics remain available but lighter action expert or gate handles the frequent control path. This design keeps qψ cheap and embodiment-specific. But the risk is equally clear, since the skipped future can help only if the retained state still preserves the part of the forecast that action needs. 4.3.4 Action representation and chunk size Across the three coupling families, the action variable at is parameterized differently. Three representations are common. The first is per-step continuous controls, with = Rda for joint velocities, end-effector deltas, or gripper commands. The second is discrete tokens from fixed codebook, as in autoregressive joint denoisers such as WorldVLA [14], where is finite vocabulary obtained from per-dimension binning. The third is learned latent actions that act as currency between the world model and the policy. In that case = Rdz for some learned dz da, as in Motus [10], ALAM [149], and LDA-1B [112]. The chunk size is the second nested choice. model that produces one action per forward pass pays low per-action latency but invokes the full backbone at the control frequency. model that produces long chunk per pass spreads the backbone cost over the chunk but cannot react during it. Closed-loop control on real hardware pushes methods toward smaller chunks or toward cheaper backbones, and many of the architectural choices in Section 4.4 can be reinterpreted as response to that pressure.

sections.title:
4.4 Architectural Backbone: How the Prediction Is Produced
Once a substrate space S is fixed and a coupling factorization is chosen, the next ingredient is the function
family that realizes pθ(· | c). We group current backbones into five families, and for each we list the defining
parameterization, the canonical training objective, and the WAMs that use it.
sections.content: 4.4.1 Iterative-denoising backbones (video diffusion) The diffusion family writes pθ as the marginal of learned reverse process over sequence of progressively denoised latents s(Ndiff), s(Ndiff1), . . . , s(0) = st+1:t+H : pθ(st+1:t+H c) = (cid:90) p(s(Ndiff)) (cid:16) pθ Ndiff(cid:89) n=1 s(n1) s(n), (cid:17) ds(Ndiff:1), (21) where p(s(Ndiff)) is Gaussian noise prior and each reverse step is parameterized by noise-prediction network ϵθ(s(n), n, c). Training minimizes the standard score-matching loss Ldiff(θ) = n,s(0),ϵ (cid:104)(cid:13) (cid:13)ϵ ϵθ (cid:16) s(n), n, 2(cid:105) (cid:17) (cid:13) (cid:13) , s(n) = αns(0) + σnϵ. (22) At inference, sampling draws s(Ndiff) p(s(Ndiff)) and runs Ndiff reverse steps. Founding video-diffusion backbones. The dominant topology is video diffusion transformer that attends jointly over space and time, as in Latte [114], CogVideoX [185], Wan [156], and Cosmos-Predict2 [128]. AnimateDiff [53] represents an earlier generation that adapts frozen image-diffusion backbone with learned temporal motion module. 21 Because standard diffusion denoises whole window at once rather than advancing one element at time, the coupling-type landscape is narrower than the autoregressive one. The census mainly uses diffusion in three ways: (Y, cdiff ) = (st+1:t+H , (c, at:t+H1)) , ((st+1:t+H , at:t+H1), c) , (st+1:t+H , c) followed by qψ(at:t+H1 st+1:t+H , c), post-prediction head. action-conditioned rollout, joint generation, (23) Pure diffusion supports action-conditioned rollout most naturally when the action source proposes whole candidate chunk before the reverse chain begins, as in DreamAvoid [33]. Step-wise rollout is less natural for windowed reverse chain, so it appears mainly in non-census foundation or simulator works such as AdaWorld [40] and FlowDreamer [50], or in hybrid wrappers that alternate an action source and predictor. Post-prediction head (Eq. 12). Many diffusion WAMs first denoise the substrate s(0) and then decode action from it with separate module qψ. The important variation is which substrate qψ consumes, not the mere fact that the action head is separate. Decoded-observation members use the generated visual future as the common interface. UniPi [29] is the representative case, since text-conditioned diffusion rollout is followed by inverse dynamics. VLP [30] changes the conditioning context before sampling, while AVDC [81], Dreamitate [99], This&That [157], Gen2Act [9], and GR-MG [89] recover actions, tool motion, or goals from the produced frames. Later visual planners mostly strengthen the generator or the search wrapper: RoboEnvision [184], Vidar [37], MVISTA-4D [160], LVP [17], Veo-Act [199], and Say-Dream-Act [48] keep the same factorization while changing horizon, view structure, executor, or sampling schedule. Pixel-latent and geometric variants keep the post-head order but make the substrate cheaper or closer to control. ARDuP [65], VILP [178], DiT4DiT [113], and OmniVTA [206] act from latent video, intermediate denoising states, or visual-tactile decoder-bound latents. Im2Flow2Act [177] is the representative geometric instance because it denoises object flow as the action-facing future. 3DFlowAction [207] and TesserAct [202] extend the same idea to 3D flow or reconstructed point clouds.
