The trade-off is direct: these substrates reduce appearance burden, but their usefulness depends on whether the chosen geometry captures the task-relevant contact and motion. Feature-level post heads push the shortcut further by consuming latent states rather than decoded futures. VPP [62], Video Policy [100], Genie Envisioner [102], Act2Goal [208], WAV [91], VAG [85], EnerVerse [66], VidMan [170], mimic-video [132], S-VAM [181], and Fast-WAM [193] use variations of this shortcut, such as tapping intermediate denoising states, distilling foresight features, fixing small number of denoising steps, or bypassing explicit video generation at inference. Related compact-substrate variants, such as MWM [109], pursue similar latency goal through task maps rather than through pure feature tap. Joint generation (Eq. 11). The second group of diffusion WAMs models the future substrate and the future action within coupled denoising or flow-matching process, so Equation 21 operates on the joint variable (s, a). PAD [52] is compact image-action DiT instance, while VideoVLA [140], CosmosPolicy [79], CoVAR [183], UD-VLA [19], GigaWorld-Policy [186], and CKT-WAM [75] vary the shared denoising state through pretrained video trunk, bridge attention, discrete tokens, action-centered masking, or transfer prefixes. GigaWorld-Policy is the action-centered case in this group: its causal mask prevents future-video tokens from influencing action tokens, so deployment can sample the action chunk without instantiating future-video tokens. AIM [32], X-WAM [51], and AdaWorldPolicy [191] show the same diffusion coupling with value maps, multi-view 4D video, or force-prediction modules attached to the joint state. The benefit is coupled generative process that encourages consistency between action and future prediction. The trade-off is potential training instability because the generation and action losses can impose competing demands on the shared representation. Practitioners therefore often pretrain the substrate head on video and introduce the action head later, sometimes with careful λ schedule. Flow matching as variant of Equation 21. large subset of the diffusion family instantiates ϵθ as velocity-field predictor under flow matching rather than as noise predictor under score matching, but both still satisfy the iterative-reverse-chain parameterization of Equation 21. Conditional flow matching learns vθ(xt, t, c) to , and at inference regress the linear interpolation velocity x1 x0 under LFM = (x1 x0) vθ(xt, t, c)2 2 22 the same Euler reverse integration takes the place of the noise-prediction reverse chain. The flow-matching variant is therefore not new backbone family but different choice of the score parameterization inside Equation 21. Among the post-prediction-head WAMs above, 3D-FDP [126], EVA [162], VAMPO [42], and MoLA [96] run flow matching as the score parameterization. Among the joint-denoising WAMs, DriveVA [106], ActionImages [203], MV-VDP [90], and CreFlow [125] take the same route on Wan-class trunk. The choice between score matching and flow matching remains separable from the coupling family within Equation 21. 4.4.2 Autoregressive backbones (next-frame, next-token) The autoregressive family is backbone parameterization, not an action-coupling regime by itself. It realizes whichever generative factor from Section 4.3 is assigned to it by serializing that factors output into causal stream. Let y1:M = Serialize(Y), where is the set of variables generated by the autoregressive backbone, and let car denote the local context supplied to that backbone. The generic autoregressive parameterization is (24) pθ(y1:M car) = (cid:89) j=1 pθ(yj y<j, car) , (25) where yj may be frame, latent-grid block, discrete visual token, continuous latent token, an action token, or compact latent state. Training minimizes the corresponding next-element loss Lar(θ) = (cid:88) j=1 log pθ(yj y<j, car) , (26) with cross-entropy for discrete tokens and continuous likelihood, regression, diffusion, or flow-matching loss for continuous elements. At inference, key-value cache reuses prefix computation, so each newly appended element requires one forward pass over the new tokens rather than recomputation over the whole history. The pair (Y, car) is inherited from the coupling choice rather than from autoregression itself: (Y, car) = action-conditioned rollout, (st+1:t+H , (c, at:t+H1)) or (st+k+1, (hk, at+k, c)) , joint autoregression, ((st+1:t+H , at:t+H1), c) , (st+1:t+H , c) followed by qψ(at:t+H1 st+1:t+H , c), post-prediction head. (27) Equation 27 is only backbone-level map. The full joint factorizations remain those in Section 4.3. Its purpose is to prevent the common confusion that autoregression implies joint action prediction. In many WAMs the autoregressive stream predicts only the future substrate. The future action may be supplied to the rollout, generated in the same stream, or decoded later by qψ. Autoregressive video priors (not WAMs). The following examples provide the video prior but not the WAM coupling. The list is selective rather than exhaustive, because these works cover the substrate forms that later WAMs reuse. VideoGPT [182] gives the VQ-VAE plus GPT recipe over discrete video latents. VideoPoet [82] is decoder-only multimodal video language model over tokenized visual and audio streams. NOVA [26] removes the discrete codebook and predicts continuous video latents. These works define reusable autoregressive video priors, but they are not WAMs by themselves. Earlier GAN-based video generators such as MoCoGAN [153] belong to the historical substrate discussion of Section 4.2. They are not instances of the next-element parameterization in Equation 25. Action-conditioned rollout (Eq. 10). The following methods use autoregression for action-conditioned sequential dynamics. iVideoGPT [174] brings the VideoGPT-style token stream into interactive world modeling by interleaving observations, actions, and rewards. AdaWorld [40] is the boundary case in this group. The paper calls it an autoregressive world model because inference repeats action-conditioned next-frame prediction, but each local transition is an SVD-based denoising problem. It is therefore best understood as an autoregressive rollout with diffusion transition, not as homogeneous next-token decoder. PlaNet [55], DreamerV3 [57], Dreamer 4 [58], and TransDreamer [18] are latent-imagination methods based on compact dynamics models. 23 These methods explain the sequential-dynamics side of the family, but they usually pair the dynamics model with separate actor, planner, or critic and therefore serve here as background rather than census WAMs. Joint autoregression (Eq. 11). The following methods use autoregression as the trunk for streams that include both future substrate elements and action-facing elements. WorldVLA [14] is the clearest discrete instance: it serializes language, image, and action tokens in one autoregressive token framework. PhysGen [144] is the continuous-latent counterpart, adapting the NOVA [26] autoregressive video prior to continuous physical tokens. GR-1 [172] establishes the GPT-style video-generative trunk for robot manipulation, and GR-2 [16] scales that trunk with larger video-language pretraining and robot-trajectory fine-tuning. From the backbone perspective, the shared property is causal next-element prediction. The exact action head, detokenizer, or joint-output factorization is the coupling detail handled in Section 4.3. Post-prediction head (Eq. 12). The post-head line of Equation 27 is still possible for autoregressive backbones, even when it is less common than joint token prediction. LingBot-VA [88] is the closest current member because it models visual dynamics before inverse dynamics, but it uses an autoregressive diffusion mixture-oftransformers rather than the next-element backbone of Equation 25. The distinction matters because the backbone can be causal while the action interface remains separate decoder over the predicted substrate. The common pressure across the family is serial cost. Autoregression avoids the many denoising steps of diffusion and benefits from caching, but it still advances the generated stream one element at time. For WAMs, the relevant latency is therefore governed by what the stream contains: full frames, latent blocks, visual tokens, action tokens, or compact latent states. When an autoregressive WAM misses real-time control budget, the first places to inspect are the serialized substrate size, the action-chunk length, and whether actions are produced inside the same causal stream or decoded by cheaper head. 4.4.3 Joint-embedding-predictive backbones The joint-embedding-predictive family realizes pθ(st+1:t+H c) as Dirac at deterministic predictor rather than as stochastic density. It learns context encoder Ectx and an exponential-moving-average target encoder Etgt together with predictor pred that maps context features to target features. Given context view xctx of the past and target view xtgt of the future, the objective is θ Ljepa(θ) = Exctx,xtgt (cid:104)(cid:13) (cid:13)f pred θ (cid:0)Ectx(xctx)(cid:1) sg(cid:2)Etgt(xtgt)(cid:3) (cid:13) (cid:13) 2(cid:105) , (28) with sg[] stop-gradient. Under the unified contract of Equation 8, the substrate st+1:t+H is Etgt(xtgt), and the predictive distribution pθ(st+1:t+H c) collapses to Dirac at pred (Ectx(xctx)). The JEPA backbone never inverts Etgt, which makes the inference loop cheap and is the source of the familys appeal. The backbone predicts the substrate only. Actions enter through one of two coupling patterns: θ (cid:40) (Y, cjepa) = (st+k+1, (hk, at+k, c)) at rollout step k, (st+1:t+H , c) followed by qψ(at:t+H1 st+1:t+H , c), post-prediction head. action-conditioned rollout, (29) Action-conditioned rollout treats the JEPA predictor as world model and recovers actions through planning over imagined consequences. post-prediction head predicts the full substrate window first and then decodes action from it with separate expert qψ. I-JEPA [3] introduces the architecture on images, predicting the latent representations of large masked target blocks from spatially distributed context block. A-JEPA [35] carries the same recipe to audio spectrograms under curriculum time-frequency masking strategy, and MC-JEPA [6] couples the prediction objective with optical-flow learning so that one shared encoder serves both motion and content. V-JEPA [7] and V-JEPA 2 [4] extend the idea to video through pure feature prediction, and the V-JEPA 2 action-conditioned variant shows how such features can support MPC through action-conditioned latent rollout. The tabulated WAMs use this lineage in two ways. FLARE [204] pairs deterministic JEPA predictor with downstream action expert in the post-prediction-head factorization by aligning additional DiT tokens to future-observation embeddings from frozen teacher. DAWN [110] keeps V-JEPA 2-style feature tokens inside hybrid WAM that alternates compact latent-world prediction with action denoising. The common pressure is that the embedding space has no intrinsic visual quality measure, so downstream-task accuracy becomes the only standard test of the world model. 24 4.4.4 Hybrid backbones (generative head plus action head) The hybrid family runs generative head and an action head on the same backbone. Schematically, pθ(st+1:t+H , at:t+H1 c) gθ(c) (cid:8)hs θ, ha θ (cid:9), (30) are two output heads. Training minimizes the joint where gθ is shared encoder-decoder trunk and hs θ, ha θ coupling loss of Equation 19 from Section 4.3, namely Lgen(s) + λLact(a), with the substrate head and action head sharing the trunk gθ. Because the shared trunk gθ feeds both heads in one place, the hybrid family is the backbone in which every coupling family of Section 4.3 can appear, and which one given model realizes is fixed by how the substrate head hs θ and the action head ha θ are ordered around that trunk: (hs θ, ha θ ) = (s, a) jointly from gθ(c), from hs qψ(a c) or external search, then ps θ, then pa θ (a s, c) , θ(s c, a), joint generation, post-prediction head, action-conditioned rollout. (31) and pa θ denote the substrate-prediction and action-prediction distributions implemented by hs θ Here ps and ha θ θ when shared trunk feeds multiple heads. The action-producing couplings place the action inside or after the shared pass, whereas action-conditioned rollout wraps the same trunk with planner, sampler, tree search, or feedback-guided action proposal. Joint generation (Eq. 11). Joint generation is the native arrangement of the family, because single pass of gθ(c) produces the future substrate and the future action under the loss of Equation 19. The plain shared-trunk form is easiest to see in UVA [93]: the video head shapes the representation during training, and the action head can be used alone at inference. UWM [213], DUST [171], VTAM [192], DDP [64], DriveDreamer-Policy [212], and DriveWAM [141] keep that contract while changing the substrate from pixel-decodable latents to VLM tokens, tactile force proxies, compact latent states, or driving-specific video and depth. Mixture-of-Transformer variants keep joint generation but separate expert routes so that video and action do not have to share every layer. F1 [111] is the representative mixture case, with generation and action experts coupled by next-scale prediction. Motus [10], MotuBrain [121], X-WAM [51], and RynnVLA-002 [13] vary the same idea through optical-flow latent actions, Vidu latents, asynchronous denoising, or an autoregressive-image plus continuous-action split. BagelVLA [63], CLWM [27], Pelican-Unify 1.0 [198], DAWN [110], NoiseGate [67], WALL-WM [92], and τ0-WM [209] then vary the schedule and exchange pattern through same-timestep attention, feature flow matching, iterative refinement, per-latent timestep gates, depth-wise cross-attention, or propose-rank-rectify control. FFDC-WAM [161] sits at the boundary between cached chunked rollout and closed-loop re-planning: the Motus joint rollout remains the action-facing substrate, while lightweight verifier decides when cached imagination should be trusted. Across the group, the shared benefit is consistency between the predicted world and the chosen action. The cost is that the action and future heads must train together without one objective overwhelming the other. completes the substrate window before ha θ Post-prediction head (Eq. 12). different arrangement keeps the shared trunk but orders the heads, so decodes the action from it. FRAPPE [200] is the teacherhs θ feature representative, routing multiple predicted future embeddings into one action transformer. Genie Envisioner [102], GigaBrain-0.5M* [158], and LaMP [164] instead expose video-trained latent, value, or motionexpert states to the action expert. LingBot-VA [88] and HarmoWAM [36] keep visual-dynamics branch active but hand frequent control to inverse-dynamics or gated action experts. Dex4D [83] and DexWM [44] show the same order on geometric or DINO-latent futures. The action expert here conditions only on the predicted substrate, so it stays cheap and can be retrained per embodiment without touching the trunk, provided that substrate is action-informative. Action-conditioned rollout (Eq. 10). Action-conditioned rollout enters the family when an action source is specified before or during consequence prediction. 3D-ALP [142] is the pixel-grounded tree-search version, where MCTS proposes joint actions, kinematics converts them into 3D camera poses, and 3D-consistent world model renders candidate futures for scoring. PointWorld [68] is the geometric chunk-level version, 25 composing an MPPI sampler over end-effector trajectories with an action-conditioned predictor over robot point-flow substrate. EgoExo-WM [152] uses horizon-length body-motion candidates from UniEgoMotion to condition DINOv3-L latent predictor under MPC. Feedback-WM [2] uses the retained predictor to guide or correct proposed action chunks after the action source has formed them. The retained world-prediction module therefore scores or corrects proposed actions instead of producing them as an unconstrained action head. Across the hybrid backbone family, the main cost is maintaining both world-prediction path and an action path in the same runtime design. The benefit depends on the coupling. Joint generation encourages consistency between predicted futures and actions within coupled generative process. Post-prediction heads keep the action expert relatively cheap once the substrate has been produced. Action-conditioned rollout supports counterfactual scoring, but repeated candidate evaluation can dominate inference. For joint members, the main optimization challenge is that substrate and action objectives can impose competing demands on the shared trunk. Practitioners therefore often pretrain the substrate path on video before introducing the action path, sometimes with scheduled weight on the action loss in Equation 19. 4.4.5 LLMand VLM-backbone WAMs The fifth family builds WAM directly on large language or vision-language backbone without using video-generation trunk as the main policy backbone. The natural substrate is the VLM-token variant of the feature category in Equation 14, since the future is described as token block in the VLMs vocabulary, and several members of the family reach across into the geometric or affordance categories of Section 4.2. The backbone is the VLM itself. The contract of Equation 8 is realized by composing VLM forward pass with an optional action expert under the post-prediction-head factorization: st+1:t+H VLMθ(prompt(c)) , at:t+H1 qψ( st+1:t+H , c) . (32) The factorization in Equation 32 realizes WAM when future substrate is part of the control pathway, either produced by the LLM/VLM itself or supplied by an attached world-model module before action decoding. VLM policy that maps observations directly to action tokens, without predicting or consuming such future substrate, falls outside this family under our definition. This subsection therefore covers LLMand VLM-backbone designs in which future tokens, subgoal images, motion representations, affordance maps, or related latent transitions are present in the inference-time control context. Among the π-series models, π0.7 [72] satisfies this gate by conditioning the policy on generated subgoal images. At runtime, lightweight BAGEL-initialized world model produces multi-view near-future visual subgoals, which are encoded together with observations by the Gemma3-based VLA before its 860M-parameter flowmatching action expert predicts the action chunk. CoT-VLA [201] writes an explicit visual chain-of-thought into the substrate before action is decoded. LDA-1B [112] learns dynamics, policy, and forecasting jointly in structured DINO-based latent on Qwen3-VL backbone. HiF-VLA [103] adopts pretrained VLA trunk with DINOv2 and SigLIP encoders that produces both motion-vector substrate tokens and action latents in one inference pass. PALM [107] carries the family down to GPT-2-class transformer over CLIP text, MAE vision, and MLP-encoded state, with diffusion transformer attached as the action expert. ICLR-VR [124] adopts Llama2-style causal transformer with pretrained ViT image encoder and decodes future-third-view gripper-keypoint polyline as the structured substrate before the action chunk fires. ALAM [149] pairs PaliGemma-2B trunk with Gemma-300M flow-matching expert, with an algebraic consistency regulariser living entirely in the frozen pretraining encoder. These examples keep the VLM family inside the WAM gate because the future substrate remains in the inference forward path. The whole family inherits the cost of the VLM stack rather than that of video diffusion, which is why many members use action chunking and frozen backbone at inference. 4.4.6 The five families together The five families are not mutually exclusive and the dividing lines move every few months. Equations 21, 25, 28, 30, and 32 are the canonical parameterizations and they appear across substrates. practitioner who fixes the substrate in Section 4.2 has narrowed the backbone choices by construction, since not every substrate 26 is compatible with every family. The remaining freedom is mostly about where the prediction lands relative to the control loop, which is the subject of Section 4.5.

sections.title:
4.5 Deployment Regime: Interactive, Rollout, Open Loop, Closed Loop
The final ingredient is the regime the WAM is meant to run in. It is the choice of horizon H in Equation 8
and of the cadence with which the WAM is invoked relative to the control loop. Let T be the task length in
control steps, let Nfwd(H) be the cost of one forward pass that produces a substrate trajectory of length H,
and let fctrl be the control frequency. The four regimes below differ primarily in how they allocate Nfwd over
the task of length T and how frequently new observations can revise the current plan.
sections.content: 4.5.1 Open-loop rollout Open-loop rollout sets and invokes the WAM once. long window of future substrate is generated once before execution and separate actor consumes it. Total compute spent on the WAM is Copen(T ) = Nfwd(T ), (33) which is paid once per scenario and does not scale with fctrl. UniPi [29], AVDC [81], Dreamitate [99], This&That [157], ARDuP [65], Gen2Act [9], RoboEnvision [184], LVP [17], and MVISTA-4D [160] run the WAM open-loop in their original setting, with the actor consuming the offline rollout through tracking or inverse dynamics. PointWorld [68] marks the open-loop limit of planning: MPPI evaluates chunked point-flow predictions over the horizon before execution, but the reported experiments do not replan during execution. Data-generation uses of Cosmos-Transfer1 [129] and Sora-class video models [130, 131] illustrate related but non-census use of generated futures, where the future model supplies synthetic experience rather than acting as an inference-time WAM. The benefit of the regime is that the cost is paid once per scenario. Its main limitation is that the rollout cannot react to real-world deviations, so the actor that consumes it must absorb every distribution shift on its own. 4.5.2 Chunked closed-loop control Chunked closed-loop sets = for some replan period and invokes the WAM every control steps. The WAM produces an action chunk of length K, the actor executes the chunk in K/fctrl seconds, and the next invocation begins. Total compute over task of length is Cchunk(T, K) = /K Nfwd(K), (34) which interpolates between open-loop (K ) and single-step (K 1). It is feasible as long as Nfwd(K)/K < 1/fctrl, so that the next chunk is prepared before the previous one is consumed. This is common choice for real-robot deployment because it amortizes large backbone over multiple control ticks. VLM-stack chunked controllers. In VLM-stack WAMs, chunked control appears when future-substrate token block or structured future is refreshed before an action chunk is decoded. π0.7 [72] produces chunked action from its Gemma3 flow-matching expert after separate BAGEL-class world model refreshes its multi-view subgoal images every four seconds, making it the π-series member that satisfies the inference-time gate. CoT-VLA [201] writes visual chain-of-thought before each chunk is decoded. HiF-VLA [103] models motion-vector substrate tokens and action latents in parallel on pretrained VLA trunk. PALM [107] and ICLR-VR [124] place diffusion or autoregressive action expert on top of an affordance map or future-thirdview gripper-keypoint polyline substrate. ALAM [149] uses algebraically consistent latent transitions on PaliGemma-family trunk. FRAPPE [200], LDA-1B [112], DUST [171], FLARE [204], and GR-2 [16] also use chunked control, with the substrate supplied by future embedding, dual-stream diffusion head, or autoregression over frame and action tokens. Pixel-latent and feature chunked controllers. WAMs that avoid decoded observation output use the same amortization principle, but differ in how much of the future substrate remains active at inference. UVA [93] and UWM [213] are the representative head-skipping cases, because the video side helps training while the inference call can produce only the action chunk. F1 [111], Motus [10], MotuBrain [121], BagelVLA [63], NoiseGate [67], Pelican-Unify 1.0 [198], τ0-WM [209], and WALL-WM [92] keep the same chunked regime inside MoT trunks. 27 Genie Envisioner [102], mimic-video [132], RynnVLA-002 [13], UD-VLA [19], and CKT-WAM [75] show adjacent ways to reduce per-call work, through slow latent refresh, intermediate features, continuous or discrete joint autoregression, or frozen student with updated context tokens. VideoVLA [140], CoVAR [183], CosmosPolicy [79], AdaWorldPolicy [191], DriveVA [106], DriveWAM [141], and DriveDreamer-Policy [212] keep chunked joint generation active in manipulation or driving settings. Other chunked designs place the chunk behind planning or abstraction stage rather than bare action head. VLP [30] is an early decoded-video case, using value-guided search over abstract actions and replanning before goal-conditioned policies execute low-level controls. ActionImages [203], MV-VDP [90], CreFlow [125], and EVA [162] decode chunks from pixel-substrate plans. MoLA [96], VAMPO [42], LaMP [164], CLWM [27], DDP [64], and DAWN [110] do the same from latent or JEPA-style substrate plans. Among action-conditioned rollouts, 3D-ALP [142], DreamAvoid [33], and Feedback-WM [2] keep the execution window chunked, but differ in whether candidates come from tree search, critical-phase sampling, or feedback-guided denoising. EgoExoWM [152] is the horizon-ranking boundary case, since it scores chunk-length body-motion candidates but reports open-loop rollout evaluation. JOPAT [49] and 3D-FDP [126] move the chunk to joint track or 3D-flow substrates, while Vidar [37], GR-MG [89], VILP [178], VideoPolicy [100], Act2Goal [208], Im2Flow2Act [177], TesserAct [202], and 3DFlowAction [207] show that the same chunked-cost equation can sit on pixel-grounded, feature, or geometric futures. Compact or optional-substrate chunked controllers. Several chunked WAMs reduce the per-call burden by making the future sparse, latent, or optional. Veo-Act [199] and Say-Dream-Act [48] still decode from generated visual plans, while VAG [85], S-VAM [181], DiT4DiT [113], Fast-WAM [193], WAV [91], and DexWM [44] move the action path to feature or teacher-target futures. MWM [109] and AIM [32] replace appearance with task maps, and GigaWorld-Policy [186] and X-WAM [51] keep joint video-action model but let the inference action path avoid the full visual schedule when possible. Variants of chunked closed-loop control. Some WAMs keep Equation 34 but make the chunk schedule task-aware. FFDC-WAM [161] is the representative adaptive case, since lightweight verifier compares predicted and observed latents and triggers an early replan when imagination drifts. NoiseGate [67] adapts denoising increments, WALL-WM [92] ties the chunk window to the next semantic event, and PALM [107] gates subtask progress inside the chunk. HarmoWAM [36] splits reactive and predictive loops, while SWEET [143] plus BagelVLA [63] reduce the substrate per call to task-critical keyframes. These variants still pay the chunked cost, but they spend it when the forecast is likely to change the action. The benefit of the chunked regime is that it spreads the cost of large backbone over the chunk and tolerates moderate backbone latency. Its main limitation is the staleness of long chunk, which becomes visible when the world changes during chunk execution. 4.5.3 Single-step closed-loop control Single-step closed-loop sets = 1 and invokes the WAM at every control step. The model produces one substrate prediction and one action per call, and per-step compute is Csingle(T ) = Nfwd(1), (35) subject to the hard constraint Nfwd(1) < 1/fctrl. The iVideoGPT [174] antecedent illustrates the autoregressive version of this regime, while current census members such as WorldVLA [14], VidMan [170], GR-1 [172], PAD [52], VPP [62], DreamZero [188], PhysGen [144], and OmniVTA [206] sit closest to the single-step WAM pattern. Dex4D [83] runs one forward pass per control tick on the student backbone to output one action and one next-joint prediction, with the goal pointer advancing only when the point-to-point distance falls below threshold. The latent-imagination Dreamer line [18, 57, 58] remains background for this regime when the agent acts at every imagination step. The regime gives the highest reactivity but pays the highest per-step inference cost. It is feasible mainly when the substrate has been chosen sparsely, in the feature, geometric, or affordance categories of Section 4.2, or in the pixel-decodable latent variant of the pixel-grounded category. In that case one forward pass can fit inside the control period. 4.5.4 Interactive simulator operation The fourth regime is the truly interactive simulator, in which stream of user or actor inputs continuously shapes the ongoing generation and there is no fixed endpoint. The WAM is invoked at every step but reuses the past via key-value cache or persistent latent of state size M(t). Per-step compute is Cint(t) = cached fwd (M(t)) , (36) where M(t) grows with the horizon. For attention-based backbones, the cached forward pass is linear in M(t) at each step and cumulative cost is quadratic in t. InteractiveWorldSimulator [166], EnerVerse [66], and LingBot-VA [88] approach this regime among the cited interactive works, while Dreamer 4 [58] remains background lineage point for persistent latent imagination. The latent-imagination lineage from PlaNet [55] and DreamerV3 [57] through TransDreamer [18] also fits when the actor and the world model run inside shared rollout loop. The defining property is that the model can be steered mid-stream and the same cache or persistent state carries the long-horizon trajectory. The cost pressure lands on memory rather than on per-step compute. Section 5 returns to that pressure under the heading of persistence.

sections.title: 4.6 Putting the Four Ingredients Together
sections.content: Sections 4.1 through 4.5 let us pin down any existing WAM by compact 4-tuple WAM = (cid:0) Φ, F, B, (cid:1), (37) whose four coordinates take values matching the table columns below. The substrate Φ from Section 4.2 takes one of {pixel-grounded, feature, geometric, affordance}. The table cells record narrower variants where they matter, with decoded or latent for pixel-grounded entries, audio-latent for the acoustic observation entry, and encoder-only, tap, teacher, or VLM token for feature. Here Pixel (latent) abbreviates the pixel-decodable latent route above. The action coupling from Section 4.3 takes one of {action-conditioned rollout, joint generation, post-prediction head}. The backbone from Section 4.4 takes one of {diffusion, autoregressive, joint-embedding, hybrid, LLM/VLM}. The deployment regime from Section 4.5 takes one of {open, chunked, single, interactive}. Example placements show how the tuple is used. F1 [111] is (pixel-decodable latent grid, joint generation, hybrid, chunked). WorldVLA [14] is (pixel-decodable latent grid, joint generation, autoregressive, single). FLARE [204] is (latent teacher-target, post-prediction head, joint-embedding, chunked). UWM [213] is (pixel-decodable latent grid, joint generation, hybrid, chunked). Frame-level video-diffusion works such as Wan [156] and CogVideoX [185] are not WAMs on their own because they fix only Φ and B. They become WAM only once and are added, which is exactly the wrapper-around-a-frozen-backbone pattern that runs through CosmosPolicy [79], VidMan [170], and VideoVLA [140] on top of related backbones. The 4-tuple also clarifies the current direction of WAM design. The first and third coordinates, Φ and B, receive substantial attention because they are training-time choices and because choosing pretrained backbone is often the expensive design decision. The middle coordinate and the closing coordinate are more flexible at inference time, and this flexibility is where many recent mechanisms appear. The census reinforces this asymmetry. Substrate choices remain inside the four categories and backbone choices cluster around diffusion and hybrid families, while sharper variation appears in coupling mechanisms such as iterative-interactive refinement, layer-wise integration, and asynchronous-adaptive denoising, and in runtime mechanisms such as adaptive-K chunking, event-triggered invocation, and dual-frequency slow-fast loops. The expensive corner of the design space combines decoded pixel-grounded substrate, joint generation, diffusion backbone, and single-step closed-loop deployment. Few methods can use that corner directly in production. Designs that appear to use it usually trade one coordinate for simulation, an offline rollout, or smaller model used at inference. Section 5 examines the same methods through the five properties that embodiment demands of them. Section 6 examines the data and the evaluation practices the field has settled on to make these design choices empirically meaningful.
